paper_title: Learning to Hint for Reinforcement Learning

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) is widely used for reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards, but it often suffers from advantage collapse: when all rollouts in a group receive the same reward, the group yields zero relative advantage and thus no learning signal. For example, if a question is too hard for the reasoner, all sampled rollouts can be incorrect and receive zero reward. Recent work addresses this issue by adding hints or auxiliary scaffolds to such hard questions so that the reasoner produces mixed outcomes and recovers a non-zero update. However, existing hints are usually fixed rather than adapted to the current reasoner, and a hint that creates learning signal under the hinted input does not necessarily improve the no-hint policy used at test time. To this end, we propose Hint Learning for Reinforcement Learning (HiLL), a framework that jointly trains a hinter policy and a reasoner policy during RL. For each hard question, the hinter generates hints online conditioned on the current reasoner's incorrect rollout, allowing hint generation to adapt to the reasoner's evolving errors. We further introduce hint reliance, which measures how strongly correct hinted trajectories depend on the hint. We derive a transferability result showing that lower hint reliance implies stronger transfer from hinted success to no-hint success, and we use this result to define a transfer-weighted reward for training the hinter. Therefore, HiLL favors hints that not only recover informative GRPO groups, but also produce signals that are more likely to improve the original no-hint policy. Experiments across multiple benchmarks show that HiLL consistently outperforms GRPO and prior hint-based baselines, demonstrating the value of adaptive and transfer-aware hint learning for RL. The code is available at https://github.com/Andree-9/HiLL.

sections.title: Start
sections.content: Yu Xia1 Canwen Xu2 Zhewei Yao2 Julian McAuley1 Yuxiong He2 1University of California, San Diego 2Snowflake AI Research 1{yux078,jmcauley}@ucsd.edu 2{canwen.xu,zhewei.yao,yuxiong.he}@snowflake.com 6 2 0 A 1 ] . [ 1 8 9 6 0 0 . 4 0 6 2 : r a

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) is widely used for reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards, but it often suffers from advantage collapse: when all rollouts in group receive the same reward, the group yields zero relative advantage and thus no learning signal. For example, if question is too hard for the reasoner, all sampled rollouts can be incorrect and receive zero reward. Recent work addresses this issue by adding hints or auxiliary scaffolds to such hard questions so that the reasoner produces mixed outcomes and recovers non-zero update. However, existing hints are usually fixed rather than adapted to the current reasoner, and hint that creates learning signal under the hinted input does not necessarily improve the no-hint policy used at test time. To this end, we propose Hint Learning for Reinforcement Learning (HiLL), framework that jointly trains hinter policy and reasoner policy during RL. For each hard question, the hinter generates hints online conditioned on the current reasoners incorrect rollout, allowing hint generation to adapt to the reasoners evolving errors. We further introduce hint reliance, which measures how strongly correct hinted trajectories depend on the hint. We derive transferability result showing that lower hint reliance implies stronger transfer from hinted success to no-hint success, and we use this result to define transfer-weighted reward for training the hinter. Therefore, HiLL favors hints that not only recover informative GRPO groups, but also produce signals that are more likely to improve the original no-hint policy. Experiments across multiple benchmarks show that HiLL consistently outperforms GRPO and prior hint-based baselines, demonstrating the value of adaptive and transfer-aware hint learning for RL. The code is available at https://github.com/Andree-9/HiLL.

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) has become standard approach to improve large language model (LLM) reasoning, especially in domains such as mathematics where finalanswer correctness can be checked reliably [3, 27]. Among recent methods, Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) [23] is widely used, which removes the value critic and estimates advantages directly from group of sampled rollouts. However, under binary outcome rewards, this design introduces basic failure mode: if all rollouts in group receive the same reward, then their relative advantages are all zero and the question produces no policy gradient [8, 12, 17, 28]. This issue appears at both ends of the difficulty spectrum. Easy questions often yield all-correct groups, while hard questions often yield all-incorrect groups. In both cases, no learning signal is obtained and no update is made. Moreover, for hard questions that the current reasoner policy cannot solve at all, standard online RL has no effective way to improve as no correct trajectory is observed and no reward signal is available [1, 21]. Therefore, the questions that matter most for expanding the models reasoning ability are often exactly the ones that provide no learning signal. To whom correspondence should be addressed. Work done during Yu Xias internship at Snowflake. Preprint. growing body of work tries to address this issue. One line of work allocates more rollouts to hard questions in order to recover rare successes and reduce variance under fixed compute budget [28, 33]. Another line filters, skips, downsamples, or reshapes uninformative groups to reduce wasted computation [8, 29, 34, 39]. third line changes the input itself by adding hints, privileged prefixes, or reasoning scaffolds to hard questions so as to induce mixed rollout outcomes and recover non-zero GRPO signals [1, 12, 35, 37]. These methods are effective in different settings, but they address different aspects of the problem. More sampling may recover signal at high cost, while fixed prompt interventions may create signal without being matched to the current reasoner. In this paper, we focus on the hint-based direction and argue that two questions are central. First, can hints adapt to the reasoners current failure modes, rather than being fixed in advance? Second, even if hint creates mixed outcomes and restores GRPO signal, does that learning signal actually help the no-hint policy used at test time? Existing hint-based methods typically rely on partial solutions, handcrafted scaffolds, or offline generated hints [1, 37], which are fixed rather than tailored to the current reasoner. In addition, they mainly value hint for turning an all-incorrect question into mixed-outcome group. However, not every such hint is useful. hint may produce correct hinted rollouts simply by making the problem much easier, without teaching the reasoner behavior that remains useful when the hint is removed. In other words, creating signal is not enough: the signal should also transfer back to the original question. We address both issues with our proposed Hint Learning for Reinforcement Learning (HiLL) framework, which treats hint generation itself as learnable objective. HiLL jointly trains hinter policy together with the reasoner during RL. For each hard question, the hinter generates hints online by conditioning on the question, an incorrect rollout from the current reasoner, and the reference solution. This enables the hinter to adapt to the reasoners evolving errors over training, rather than relying on static offline hints that may stop being useful as the reasoner changes. Moreover, HiLL provides transfer-aware view of hint quality. good hint should do more than create non-zero GRPO update: it should guide the reasoner toward correct trajectories that are still plausible under the original no-hint question. If correct hinted trajectory remains likely even after the hint is removed, then training on that trajectory is more likely to improve the no-hint policy. By contrast, if success depends strongly on the hint, then the hint acts more like shortcut than useful teaching signal. To capture this distinction, we introduce hint reliance, which measures how much correct hinted trajectories depend on the hint relative to the original question. We then derive transferability result showing that lower hint reliance implies stronger transfer from hinted success to no-hint success. Based on this result, we define transfer-weighted reward for the hinter training, encouraging hints that both create informative GRPO groups and produce learning signals that are more likely to transfer and help the reasoner improve in the no-hint scenario. Therefore, HiLL turns hinting for RL into an online co-training problem between hinter policy and reasoner policy. As training progresses, previously hard questions may become solvable, while newly difficult questions define the reasoners current capability boundary. The hinter is thus trained on an evolving distribution of reasoner failures and learns to adapt its generated hints to the reasoners changing weaknesses. This makes hinting dynamic part of the RL process rather than fixed preprocessing step. In summary, we make the following contributions: We propose HiLL, co-training framework that jointly optimizes hinter policy and reasoner policy, enabling online hint generation conditioned on the current reasoners failures. We introduce hint reliance as measure of whether hinted success is likely to transfer back to the original no-hint setting, derive transferability result based on this quantity, and use it to construct transfer-weighted reward for training the hinter policy. We show on comprehensive benchmarks that HiLL consistently outperforms standard GRPO and hint-based baselines, demonstrating the value of adaptive and transfer-aware hint learning for RL.

sections.title: 2 Related Work
sections.content: Sampling and filtering strategies for RLVR. When GRPO rollout groups are uniformly correct or incorrect, within-group advantages collapse and the question yields no gradient [8, 12, 17, 28]. Adaptive sampling methods address this by reallocating rollout budgets toward questions near the models capability boundary, e.g., via variance-minimizing allocation [33], adaptive importance2 Figure 1: Overview of our HiLL framework. Given question with an all-incorrect group, the hinter Hϕ takes the question, failed rollout τk, and the reference solution as input, and generates candidate hints. The reasoner πθ re-samples rollouts under each hinted input q+hj. Each hint is then scored by signal creation (Sec. 4.2) and signal transfer (Sec. 4.3). The best hinted group is selected reasoner GRPO update, while all candidate hints form the group for the hinter GRPO update. based scheduling [28], or knapsack-style budget optimization [11]. Filtering and reshaping methods take complementary approach, skipping or clipping degenerate groups [34, 39], downsampling uninformative rollouts [29], or extracting signal from zero-variance groups via entropy-guided advantage shaping [8] and virtual reward calibration [18]. Curriculum-based scheduling further improves sample efficiency by ordering questions from easy to hard or partitioning by difficulty [19, 36]. These strategies are complementary to hinting: they improve how existing signal is used but cannot create signal when the models success probability on question is effectively zero. Privileged hinting and scaffolded RL. growing line of work modifies the input during LLM training by injecting privileged information to induce correct rollouts hard questions, including oracle solution prefixes [21], self-generated hints [12], external teacher hints [37], multi-level progressive hints [35], curriculum-based scaffolds [1], or initial steps extracted from rare within-batch successes [20]. key design principle shared across these methods is that the model should be trained on-policy with respect to the augmented input, i.e., both rollout sampling and the policy-gradient loss should condition on the privileged context, not the original question alone [12, 21]. Recent work also utilizes such privileged information for on-policy self-distillation, where model generates on-policy trajectories under student context and receives dense supervision from its own privileged context conditioned on ground-truth solutions, verified reasoning traces, or rich textual feedback [6, 24, 38]. Our HiLL framework shares this on-policy structure, where privileged information reshapes on-policy trajectories during training and is removed at test time, but differs in two key respects: (i) it actively learns to generate better hints through RL during the privileged training rather than relying on fixed or pre-collected contexts, and (ii) it explicitly optimizes for transferability by penalizing hints whose induced correct trajectories are unlikely to improve the model under no-hint scenarios.

sections.title: 3 Preliminaries
sections.content: Consider reasoning with verifiable rewards. Given question q, reasoner policy πθ samples trajectory τ πθ( q), and verifier returns binary reward r(τ ) {0, 1} indicating whether the final answer is correct. We denote the per-question success probability by pθ(q) = Prτ πθ(q)[r(τ ) = 1]. GRPO and advantage collapse. Group Relative Policy Optimization [23] samples group of trajectories for the same question and computes within-group normalized advantages: Ai = ri std(r1:G) + ϵ , = 1 G (cid:88) i=1 ri. (1) 3 Under binary rewards, if all rollouts receive the same reward, every advantage vanishes and the question produces no gradient. For question with per-rollout success probability p, the probability that group of size contains both correct and incorrect outcomes is s(p; G) = 1 pG (1 p)G. (2) This non-degenerate probability is maximized at = 1 2 and satisfies s(p; G) Gp when 0. Hard questions with small success probability are therefore most likely to yield degenerate groups and produce no learning signal.

sections.title: 4 HiLL: Hint Learning for Reinforcement Learning
sections.content: HiLL jointly trains hinter policy alongside the reasoner to recover learning signal from hard questions. It intervenes only on all-incorrect GRPO groups, which produce no gradient under binary rewards. As illustrated in Figure 1 and summarized in Algorithm 1, the hinter generates candidate hints online, the reasoner re-samples under each hinted input, and hints are scored by both signal creation and signal transfer before the best hinted group replaces the original degenerate group.

sections.title: 4.1 Failure-Conditioned Hint Generation
sections.content: Given training batch B, the reasoner first samples rollouts per question and identifies the allincorrect subset = {q : (cid:80)G i=1 ri(q) = 0}. For each I, the hinter policy Hϕ generates candidate hints conditioned on the question q, randomly selected incorrect rollout τk from the current reasoners all-incorrect group, and reference solution available only during training: {hj}M j=1 Hϕ( q, τk, z). (3) The incorrect rollout exposes the reasoners current error mode, while the reference solution provides supervision for producing targeted hint. We instruct the hinter to analyze the reasoners failure and output concise pedagogical hint where the prompt is provided in Appendix B. candidate hint is invalid if it (i) violates the required output format, (ii) leaks the final answer or key intermediate computations, or (iii) causes the concatenated input q+h to exceed the maximum context length. Invalid hints receive fixed failure penalty Rfail < 0 as defined in Section 4.4. 4.2 Hinted Rollouts and Signal Creation (cid:80)G i=1 r(hj ) For each valid candidate hint hj, the reasoner re-samples rollouts from πθ( q+hj) and computes rewards. Let ˆph = 1 denote the estimated success rate under the hinted input. hint creates useful signal when the resulting group is non-degenerate, i.e., contains both correct and incorrect rollouts. We quantify this with the non-degenerate probability s(ˆph; G) = 1ˆpG (1ˆph)G from Eq. (2), which vanishes when ˆph {0, 1} and peaks at ˆph = 1 2 . However, signal creation alone is not sufficient: hint may produce mixed outcomes simply by making the problem much easier, without producing trajectories that remain useful once the hint is removed. We therefore introduce second criterion that measures whether the created signal transfers to the original no-hint question. 4.3 Hint Reliance and Signal Transfer If correct hinted trajectories depend strongly on the hint, training on them may teach the reasoner to exploit the hint rather than improve its no-hint reasoning capability. For example, if the reasoner fails to simplify key expression, hint like try factoring the left-hand side suggests strategy the model could have found on its own, whereas note that x25x+6 = (x2)(x3) performs the critical step directly for the reasoner. Correct trajectories from the latter depend largely on the hint and are unlikely to transfer. Hint reliance. To formalize this, we define the hint reliance of trajectory τ sampled under q+h as ρ(τ ; q, h) = log πθ(τ q+h) log πθ(τ q). We average over correct hinted trajectories, which carry the informative GRPO signal: ρc(q, h) = 1 (cid:88) τ ρ(τ ; q, h), = (cid:8)τ πθ( q+h) : r(τ ) = 1(cid:9). (5) The following result shows that this average hint reliance directly controls how well hinted success transfers to the no-hint setting. Proposition 1 (Transfer bound). For question-hint pair (q, h), let Ph(τ ) = πθ(τ q+h) and (τ ) = πθ(τ q) denote the rollout distributions under the hinted and original inputs, with success probabilities ph = Ph (cid:0)r(τ )=1(cid:1) and = (cid:0)r(τ )=1(cid:1). If ph > 0, then + DKL ρc(q, h) = log (cid:0)Ph( r=1) (cid:13) (cid:13) ( r=1)(cid:1), ph and therefore ph exp(cid:0)ρc(q, h)(cid:1). The proof is provided in Appendix A. The identity decomposes hint reliance into two terms: the log-ratio of success probabilities and KL divergence measuring how the distribution over correct trajectories shifts when the hint is added. Since the KL term is non-negative, the bound follows directly: the no-hint success probability is at least the hinted success probability ph discounted by exp(ρc). Therefore, lower hint reliance yields tighter bound and stronger transfer guarantee. Practical estimator. In practice, we normalize ρ(τ ; q, h) by the trajectory length τ to reduce length bias across trajectories of varying lengths: ˆρc(q, h) = 1 (cid:88) τ ρ(τ ; q, h) τ . (6) Computing ˆρc requires scoring each correct hinted trajectory under both q+h and q, amounting to two teacher-forced forward passes of πθ. 5 4.4 Transfer-Weighted Hinter Reward Building on the previous signal creation and transfer measure, we now define the reward for each candidate hint for hinter training: R(q, h) = Rfail, if is invalid, s(ˆph; G) exp (cid:18) max(cid:0)ˆρc(q, h), 0(cid:1) (cid:19) , (cid:125) (cid:123)(cid:122) (cid:124) signal creation (cid:124) (cid:123)(cid:122) signal transfer (cid:125) otherwise, (7) where > 0 is temperature parameter. The first term s(ˆph; G) from Eq. (2) rewards hints that produce mixed-outcome groups and vanishes when all rollouts are correct or all incorrect. The second term based on Eq. (6) serves as transfer weight: it equals 1 when hint reliance is zero or negative; it decays as reliance increases. Negative reliance means the correct trajectory is already at least as likely under the original question, so we treat it as fully transferable and do not further boost the reward. Their product encourages hints that both recover non-zero GRPO signal and produce trajectories that transfer to the no-hint setting. When ˆph {0, 1}, signal creation is zero, so no hint reliance computation is needed. 4. Joint Reasoner-Hinter Optimization After scoring all candidates, the best hint = arg maxj R(q, hj) is selected for each I. If R(q, h) > 0, the batch entry is updated via replacement (q, {τi}G i=1), where τ πθ( q+h). Otherwise, the original entry is kept. Reasoner update. After replacement, each question is paired with an input xq (either or q+h). Note that when hint is used, both the rollout sampling and the log-probability computation condition on the same hinted input xq = q+h, keeping the reasoner update on-policy with respect to the augmented context. The reasoner is then updated with the standard GRPO objective: i=1) (q+h, {τ }G LR(θ) = EqB (cid:34) (cid:88) τi (cid:88) Ai i=1 t=1 log πθ (cid:0)yi,t xq, yi,<t (cid:1) (cid:35) , (8) where τi πθ( xq) and Ai are within-group normalized advantages as in Eq. (1). Hinter update. For each I, the candidate hints form GRPO group with rewards {R(q, hj)}M j=1, and the hinter is updated via: (cid:34) (cid:88) LH(ϕ) = EqI hj (cid:88) Aj log Hϕ(hj,t cq, hj,<t) , (9) (cid:35) t=1 where Aj are group-normalized advantages from the hint rewards, and cq = (q, τk, z). Invalid candidates participate through Rfail, allowing the hinter to learn to avoid producing them. j=1 Co-training dynamics. Because both policies are updated jointly, the hinter adapts as the reasoner improves. Questions that become solvable leave I, while the remaining failures reflect the reasoners current capability frontier. The hinter is thus trained on an evolving distribution of reasoner failures and learns to adjust its hints to the reasoners changing weaknesses, rather than relying on fixed hinting strategy throughout training.

sections.title: 5 Experiments
sections.content: 5.1 Experimental Setup Models. We evaluate our HiLL framework on two models as the reasoner policy: Llama-3.23B-Instruct [15] and Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct [32]. The hinter policy is initialized from Qwen3-4BInstruct [31] for all experiments. Both policies are trained jointly via GRPO as described in Section 4. Training data. We use the same 15k-prompt subset of OpenR1-Math-220k [2] curated by Liao et al. [12], drawn from NuminaMath 1.5 [10] together with ground-truth answers and reference solutions. No pass-rate filtering is applied, so questions span wide difficulty range. 6 Table 1: Main results on in-distribution and out-of-distribution benchmarks. Best results are in bold and second-best are underlined. HiLL consistenctly outperforms GRPO and hint-based baselines. Method AIME24 / 25 AMC23 MATH-500 Minerva Olympiad Avg. GPQA MMLU-Pro Avg. In-distribution Out-of-distribution Llama-3.2-3B-Instruct Base GRPO LUFFY Scaf-GRPO SAGE HiLLw/o TW HiLL 6.5 / 0.6 6.7 / 0.8 4.4 / 0.4 7.7 / 2.3 9.2 / 0.8 6.9 / 2.3 8.5 / 1. Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct Base GRPO LUFFY Scaf-GRPO SAGE HiLLw/o TW HiLL 13.8 / 6.7 15.0 / 13.5 17.1 / 13.5 14.6 / 12.7 16.0 / 12.5 15.2 / 14.4 16.9 / 15.3 22.8 29.5 18.6 28.8 34.7 32.5 34.8 53.4 55.5 55.2 58.8 60.3 60.5 63.0 44.7 52.1 38.9 51.7 56.3 56.7 57. 75.7 79.2 81.3 78.0 80.0 80.5 81.8 17.8 20.5 14.3 19.4 20.1 21.6 21.7 38.1 39.1 39.0 39.8 39.3 39.8 40.1 14.2 21.8 11.9 19.5 22.0 22.4 23.2 39.2 44.5 44.2 42.0 45.9 45.7 48.1 17.8 21.9 14.7 21.5 23.9 23.7 24. 37.8 41.1 41.7 41.0 42.3 42.7 44.2 17.9 26.3 16.0 24.1 27.3 28.0 27.8 37.1 37.2 38.1 36.6 38.0 38.6 40.4 27.0 39.8 26.7 38.0 40.7 41.0 42.7 56.4 57.6 59.1 58.4 59.3 59.9 61.6 22.5 33.1 21.4 31.0 34.0 34.5 35. 46.7 47.4 48.6 47.5 48.6 49.2 51.0 Evaluation. We report Average@16 accuracy on six math reasoning benchmarks: AIME24 [13], AIME25 [14], AMC23 [10], MATH-500 [5], Minerva Math [9], OlympiadBench [4], and two non-math benchmarks: GPQA-diamond [22], MMLU-Pro [26] for generalization assessment. All reasoner models are evaluated with temperature 0.6, top-p 0.95, and maximum response length 8,192. The hinter is never used at evaluation. Baselines. We compare against the following baselines trained on the same 15k prompts: (1) Base, the initial reasoner; (2) GRPO [23], standard RL without hints; (3) LUFFY [30], which replaces one on-policy rollout with an off-policy trajectory from DeepSeek-R1; (4) Scaf-GRPO [37], which augments all-incorrect groups with hints from an external teacher model; and (5) SAGE [12], onpolicy hinting with self-generated hints conditioned on reference solutions. The implementation builds on the official codebase of Liao et al. [12], and we report baseline results from their paper. We additionally report HiLLw/o TW, an ablated variant of HiLL that removes the transfer weight from the hinter reward (Eq. (7)), using only the non-degenerate probability as the reward for hinter training. Implementation details. We use verl [25] for RL training and vLLM [7] for rollout generation and all experiments run on 8B200 GPUs. For reasoner policy training in all methods, we follow DAPO [34] to disable the KL penalty and apply Clip-Higher with ϵlow=0.2, ϵhigh=0.28. We train the reasoner for 500 steps with batch size of 128, G=8 rollouts per prompt, and learning rate 106. The maximum prompt and response lengths are set to 2,048 and 8,192 tokens. We evaluate every 50 steps and select the reasoner checkpoint with the best Average@16 accuracy to report the results. For hinter policy training in HiLL, the hinter generates =4 candidate hints per all-incorrect question with maximum prompt and response length being 10,240 and 1,024 tokens, transfer temperature =0.3, failure penalty Rfail=0.2, and the same learning rate and clipping as the reasoner. We use Ray [16] to co-locate reasoner and hinter models on the same GPU nodes. Since the co-training pipeline executes each phase sequentially as in Algorithm 1, Ray idles one policy while the other is active, letting both FSDP-sharded models share the same devices without extra memory overhead compared to baseline training methods. Prompts used for reasoner and hinter are provided in Appendix B. 5.2 Results and Analysis HiLL consistently boosts reasoner performance compared to baselines. Table 1 summarizes the main results. HiLL achieves the highest average accuracy on in-distribution math reasoning tasks on both Llama-3.2-3B-Instruct and Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct. The gains extend to out-of-distribution GPQA and MMLU-Pro despite training exclusively on math data, suggesting that the recovered learning signal in our HiLL framework also generalizes to broader reasoning capabilities. Among hint-based baselines, both Scaf-GRPO and SAGE improve over standard GRPO, confirming that hinting can recover useful signal from hard questions. HiLL outperforms both, indicating that learning to generate adaptive, transfer-aware hints is more effective than relying on fixed external hints or self-generated 7 Figure 2: All-incorrect ratio (left two) and hint reliance (right two) over training steps. Both HiLL variants substantially reduce the fraction of degenerate all-incorrect groups compared to GRPO. Transfer weighting (HiLL vs. HiLLw/o TW) keeps hint reliance consistently lower throughout training. Figure 3: Effect of transfer temperature on average signal creation, signal transfer, and indistribution accuracy for HiLL with Llama-3.2-3B-Instruct. Dashed lines show HiLLw/o TW. hints without transfer optimization. LUFFY underperforms on Llama-3.2-3B-Instruct, likely because injecting off-policy DeepSeek-R1 trajectories into smaller model introduces distribution mismatch that outweighs the benefit of additional correct rollouts. Transfer weighting keeps hint reliance low while recovering GRPO signals. Figure 2 tracks the all-incorrect ratio, i.e., the fraction of degenerate all-incorrect groups in the reasoner batch, and its hint reliance over training. Both HiLL variants substantially reduce this ratio compared to standard GRPO, confirming that learned hinting effectively recovers signal from hard questions. The key difference between the two variants appears in the hint reliance curves: without transfer weighting, the hinter is rewarded purely for creating mixed-outcome groups, and reasoner hint reliance climbs steadily as training progresses. With transfer weighting, HiLL keeps hint reliance consistently low, meaning the hinter learns to produce hints whose induced correct trajectories remain plausible under the original no-hint input. With the accuracy gains of HiLL over HiLLw/o TW in Table 1, this confirms that lower hint reliance translates into stronger transfer to the no-hint policy, as predicted by Proposition 1. Transfer temperature controls the signal creationtransfer trade-off. The temperature in hinter reward defined in Eq. (7) controls how aggressively the transfer weight penalizes hint reliance. We show in Figure 3 its effect on Llama-3.2-3B-Instruct across three metrics: (i) signal creation, i.e., the average reduction in all-incorrect ratio after hinting where higher value means more degenerate groups in reasoner batch are converted to mixed-outcome groups, (ii) signal transfer, i.e., exp(ˆρc) averaged over training where higher value means correct hinted trajectories are more likely under the original no-hint input, and (iii) in-distribution accuracy, i.e., average reasoner performance over in-distribution math benchmarks. The results show that smaller imposes stronger penalty, yielding higher signal transfer but reducing signal creation as the hinter becomes over-constrained. larger relaxes the penalty, creating more signal but with lower transferability. The choice of =0.3 in our experiments provides good balance between signal creation and signal transfer, achieving strong accuracy performance. Nevertheless, all three values tested in HiLL outperform HiLLw/o TW, indicating that even loose transfer weighting is beneficial for the reasoner training. HiLL learns to hint more concisely and conceptually. Beyond the quantitative metrics above, transfer weighting also shapes the nature of the generated hints. We report in Figure 4 the average hint length in words and the average number of math expressions (e.g., inline equations, symbols) per hint, both computed over hints collected on fixed interval of 50 steps across training. The results show that HiLL produces shorter hints with fewer math expressions than HiLLw/o TW on both backbones. Table 2 illustrates the qualitative difference with two actual examples: HiLL hints suggest 8 Table 2: Example hints generated by HiLL and HiLLw/o TW on the same question. HiLL provides conceptual strategies while HiLLw/o TW tends to set up the computation directly. Question (excerpt) Hint from HiLL Hint from HiLLw/o TW line segment AB of length 1 moves on the x-axis. Point (0, 1) is connected to by line, and point Q(1, 2) is connected to by line. Find the equation of the trajectory of the intersection point R. In trapezoid ABCD, AB CD, AB=33, CD=21, the non-parallel sides are 10 and 14, and A, are both acute. Find the length of the shorter diagonal. Consider expressing the intersection in terms of parameter a, then eliminate it to reveal the hidden trajectory. (18 words, hinted pass rate 0.13) The shorter diagonal emerges from triangle where 14, 12, and 10 form critical configuration, with the angle between them yielding result just above 25. (27 words, hinted pass rate 0.13) Let = (a, 0) and = (a+1, 0) for real parameter = 0. Write the equation of line A, where = (0, 1), and the equation of line QB, where = (1, 2), both in terms of a. Find the coordinates (x, y) of their intersection point as function of a.. . . (108 words, hinted pass rate 0.38) Draw line DE BC from point to side AB, intersecting AB at point E. This creates triangle ADE with DE = 10, AE = 12, and AD = 14. Use the Law of Cosines in triangle ADE to find cos A. Then apply the same angle in . . . (97 words, hinted pass rate 0.50) high-level strategy (e.g., parameterize then eliminate or look for critical triangle configuration) without carrying out the computation, whereas HiLLw/o TW hints tend to set up the algebra directly by defining coordinates, writing equations, or applying specific theorems. This behavior emerges naturally from our transfer-weighted hint learning objective. Hints that perform key steps for the reasoner would induce high hint reliance, as the resulting correct trajectories become unlikely under the original input. The transfer weight penalizes such hints (Eq. (7)), steering the hinter toward generating more conceptual guidance whose induced correct trajectories the reasoner could plausibly produce on its own. Figure 4: Hint length and math expressions per hint. HiLL trades off latency for more effective learning signals. HiLL introduces additional per-step computation beyond standard GRPO: for each degenerate all-incorrect group in the batch, the hinter generates hint, the reasoner re-samples trajectories conditioned on the hinted input, and hint reliance is estimated from the resulting trajectories. The hinter is then updated via its own GRPO step. Because these operations are triggered only for all-incorrect groups, the overhead scales directly with the frequency of such groups. On Llama-3.2-3B-Instruct, which has high all-incorrect ratio throughout training as shown in Figure 2, HiLL averages roughly 3.8 the per-step wall-clock time of GRPO. On the stronger Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct backbone, which produces fewer degenerate groups, the multiplier drops to roughly 2.6, comparable to SAGEs 2.3 on the same backbone, as SAGE also targets collapsed groups by progressively trying up to L=3 hint levels and rolling out at each level until correct trajectory is found. We view this as practical trade-off: the extra computation targets exactly the groups from which GRPO extracts no learning signal, converting them into useful training data that drives the accuracy gains reported in Table 1.

sections.title: 6 Conclusion
sections.content: In this paper, we introduced HiLL, co-training framework that addresses two limitations of existing hint-based approaches to GRPOs advantage collapse. First, rather than relying on fixed or externally generated hints, HiLL jointly trains hinter policy that generates hints online conditioned on the reasoners current failures, allowing hint generation to stay calibrated as the reasoner improves. Second, rather than rewarding hints solely for creating mixed-outcome groups, HiLL introduces hint reliance as measure of whether hinted success is likely to transfer back to the no-hint policy, and uses it to define transfer-weighted reward that steers the hinter toward hints whose induced correct trajectories remain plausible without the hint. Experiments across eight benchmarks and two backbone LLMs show that HiLL consistently outperforms GRPO and prior hint-based baselines. Our analysis further reveals that transfer weighting keeps hint reliance low throughout training and 9 naturally produces shorter, more conceptual hints. Together, these results demonstrate the value of adaptive and transfer-aware hint learning for RL.

sections.title: B Prompts for Hinter and Reasoner
sections.content: This section lists all prompt templates used in HiLL. We do not set system prompt for the hinter. Note that for the hinted reasoner input, we simply append the hint after the original question without adding explicit bridging prose such as Here is hint to help you: as used in Liao et al. [12]. We empirically observed that such phrasing increases hint reliance by inducing reasoner outputs like Given the hint, . . . which are less likely under the no-hint input and thus less transferable. User Prompt for Hint Generation You are Pedagogical Hint Generator for Mathematical Reasoner. TASK: GENERATE MINIMAL HINT OBJECTIVE The Reasoner failed the Original Question. Your goal is to generate minimal but useful hint that helps the Reasoner find the correct logical path. Provide strategic nudge, not solution. The Reasoner must still perform the deduction themselves. INPUTS 1) Original Question: {original_question} 2) Reasoners Failed Attempt: {rollout_responses} 3) Ground Truth Solution (Hidden from Reasoner; For Your Reference Only): {ground_truth_solution} GUIDELINES 1. Analyze the failure to identify the missing insight or misconception. 2. Provide conceptual pointer, such as relevant theorem, structural property, an alternative representation, or an intermediate goal. 3. Do not reveal the final answer or any specific numerical values, formulas with substituted numbers, or computed intermediate steps. OUTPUT FORMAT (Follow strictly) <analysis> [Briefly identify the logic breakdown and the necessary insight.] </analysis> <hint> [Your concise hint 1 to 3 sentences maximum.] </hint> System Prompt for Reasoner Please reason step by step, and put your final answer within boxed{}. User Prompt for Reasoner {question} 13 User Prompt for Reasoner with Hint {question} {hint}
